title.en: RoBERTa vs BERT: Head-to-Head Comparison and Downstream Fine-Tuning

description.en: Run BERT and RoBERTa side by side on fill-mask, feature extraction, and text classification — measure exactly where RoBERTa's training improvements translate into real downstream gains.

story_variants.en:
## RoBERTa vs BERT: Prove It With Code

Vaathiyaar here! Everyone says RoBERTa is "better than BERT" — but *how much* better, and *why*? In this lesson we won't take benchmarks on faith. We'll run both models head-to-head on fill-mask predictions, embedding quality, and downstream classification, then measure the differences ourselves. By the end, you'll know exactly when to reach for `FacebookAI/roberta-base` over `google-bert/bert-base-uncased`.

### The Training Recipe That Changed Everything

RoBERTa's architecture is identical to BERT — 12 layers, 768 hidden dimensions, 12 attention heads. The breakthrough was entirely in **how** it was trained:

| Training Decision | BERT-base | RoBERTa-base | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Training data | 16GB | 160GB (10x) | More diverse patterns learned |
| Masking | Static (fixed once) | Dynamic (re-masked each epoch) | Sees more masking variations |
| NSP objective | Yes | Removed | NSP hurt single-sentence tasks |
| Batch size | 256 | 8,000 | Better gradient estimates |
| Tokenizer | WordPiece (30K) | Byte-level BPE (50K) | No unknown tokens, preserves case |

### Side-by-Side Fill-Mask Comparison

```python
from transformers import pipeline

bert = pipeline("fill-mask", model="google-bert/bert-base-uncased")
roberta = pipeline("fill-mask", model="FacebookAI/roberta-base")

prompts = [
    ("The capital of Japan is [MASK].",
     "The capital of Japan is <mask>."),
    ("Python is a [MASK] language.",
     "Python is a <mask> language."),
]

for bert_p, roberta_p in prompts:
    b = bert(bert_p)[0]
    r = roberta(roberta_p)[0]
    print(f"BERT: {b['token_str']:>12} ({b['score']:.3f})")
    print(f"RoBERTa: {r['token_str']:>12} ({r['score']:.3f})")
    print()
```

RoBERTa consistently shows higher confidence on factual queries and handles cased proper nouns ("Tokyo" vs "tokyo") naturally.

### Embedding Quality Comparison

```python
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch
from torch.nn.functional import cosine_similarity

def get_cls_embedding(model_name, text):
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(model_name)
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)
    return outputs.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]

pairs = [
    ("The movie was great", "I loved the film"),
    ("The movie was great", "The weather is sunny"),
]

for s1, s2 in pairs:
    b1 = get_cls_embedding("google-bert/bert-base-uncased", s1)
    b2 = get_cls_embedding("google-bert/bert-base-uncased", s2)
    r1 = get_cls_embedding("FacebookAI/roberta-base", s1)
    r2 = get_cls_embedding("FacebookAI/roberta-base", s2)
    print(f"BERT sim: {cosine_similarity(b1, b2).item():.4f}")
    print(f"RoBERTa sim: {cosine_similarity(r1, r2).item():.4f}")
```

RoBERTa embeddings tend to separate semantically different sentences more cleanly — the gap between similar and dissimilar pairs is wider.

### Fine-Tuning Both for Classification

```python
from transformers import (
    AutoModelForSequenceClassification,
    AutoTokenizer,
    TrainingArguments,
    Trainer
)

def build_trainer(model_name, train_ds, eval_ds, num_labels=2):
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
        model_name, num_labels=num_labels
    )
    args = TrainingArguments(
        output_dir=f"./results-{model_name.split('/')[-1]}",
        num_train_epochs=3,
        per_device_train_batch_size=16,
        learning_rate=2e-5,
        weight_decay=0.01,
        evaluation_strategy="epoch",
        save_strategy="epoch",
        load_best_model_at_end=True,
    )
    return Trainer(model=model, args=args,
                   train_dataset=train_ds, eval_dataset=eval_ds)

# Train both with identical hyperparameters
bert_trainer = build_trainer("google-bert/bert-base-uncased",
                              train_ds, eval_ds)
roberta_trainer = build_trainer("FacebookAI/roberta-base",
                                 train_ds, eval_ds)
```

Using identical hyperparameters isolates the model quality difference. RoBERTa typically gains 1-3% accuracy on GLUE tasks with zero extra tuning effort.

### Tokenizer Differences in Practice

| Input Text | BERT Tokens | RoBERTa Tokens |
|---|---|---|
| `"ChatGPT"` | `['chat', '##gp', '##t']` | `['Chat', 'GPT']` |
| `"café"` | `['cafe', '##\u0301']` | `['caf', 'é']` |
| `"don't"` | `['don', "'", 't']` | `['don', "'t"]` |

RoBERTa's byte-level BPE never produces `[UNK]` tokens and handles mixed-case text, contractions, and Unicode more naturally.

> **Key Insight:** RoBERTa and BERT share the same transformer architecture — every performance difference comes from training methodology. Dynamic masking, NSP removal, 10x data, and byte-level BPE compound to give RoBERTa 1-3% higher accuracy on downstream tasks with zero architectural changes. When starting a new NLP project, RoBERTa-base is the strictly superior default over BERT-base.

animation_sequence.content: RoBERTa and BERT share the same transformer architecture — 12 layers, 768 dimensions, 12 attention heads. Every performance difference comes from training: dynamic masking, no NSP, 10x data, and byte-level BPE. Let's run them side by side and measure the real impact.

animation_sequence.code:
from transformers import pipeline

bert = pipeline("fill-mask", model="google-bert/bert-base-uncased")
roberta = pipeline("fill-mask", model="FacebookAI/roberta-base")

bert_result = bert("The capital of Japan is [MASK].")
roberta_result = roberta("The capital of Japan is <mask>.")

print(f"BERT:    {bert_result[0]['token_str']} ({bert_result[0]['score']:.3f})")
print(f"RoBERTa: {roberta_result[0]['token_str']} ({roberta_result[0]['score']:.3f})")

steps.explanation: Import the pipeline abstraction — it handles tokenization, model loading, and postprocessing in a single function call.
Load BERT-base-uncased (~440MB). The 'uncased' variant lowercases all input — 'Tokyo' becomes 'tokyo'.
Load RoBERTa-base (~500MB). It's cased by default and uses a different mask token: <mask> instead of [MASK].
BERT fill-mask uses [MASK]. Using the wrong mask token is a common bug — the model will silently return poor predictions instead of erroring.
RoBERTa uses <mask>. Both models predict from bidirectional context, but RoBERTa's 160GB training gives it stronger world knowledge.
BERT predicts 'tokyo' (lowercased, ~90% confidence). Its WordPiece tokenizer with 30K vocabulary handles proper nouns less gracefully.
RoBERTa predicts 'Tokyo' (properly cased, ~95%+ confidence). The 50K BPE vocabulary and cased training make proper nouns natural.

animation_sequence.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch
from torch.nn.functional import cosine_similarity

def get_cls_embedding(model_name, text):
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(model_name)
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)
    return outputs.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]

sim_r = cosine_similarity(
    get_cls_embedding("FacebookAI/roberta-base", "The movie was great"),
    get_cls_embedding("FacebookAI/roberta-base", "I loved the film")
)
print(f"RoBERTa similarity: {sim_r.item():.4f}")

steps.explanation: AutoTokenizer and AutoModel auto-detect the correct class (BERT or RoBERTa) from the model name — no need to import specific classes.
cosine_similarity measures how closely aligned two embedding vectors are. Values near 1.0 mean semantically similar, near 0 means unrelated.
Define a reusable function that extracts the CLS token embedding — the sentence-level representation at position 0 of the output.
Tokenize with return_tensors='pt' for PyTorch. BERT adds [CLS]/[SEP], RoBERTa adds <s>/</s> — AutoTokenizer handles the difference.
torch.no_grad() disables gradient tracking — essential for inference to save ~50% memory and improve speed.
Extract position 0 from last_hidden_state — the CLS/<s> token that serves as the sentence representation for downstream tasks.
Compare RoBERTa embeddings of two semantically similar sentences. Higher cosine similarity indicates the model captures meaning better.

items.left: Static masking — same masks every epoch
items.right: Dynamic masking — fresh masks each epoch
MLM only — NSP removed (it hurt performance)

animation_sequence.message: You've proven it with code — RoBERTa's training improvements deliver measurably better fill-mask, embeddings, and downstream classification over BERT!

instruction.en: Build a `ModelComparator` class that simulates running two models head-to-head on evaluation tasks. Initialize with two model names and their simulated scores. Implement `add_benchmark(name, model_a_score, model_b_score)` to record benchmark results, `get_winner(benchmark_name)` that returns the model name with the higher score (or 'tie'), `summary()` that returns a dict with 'model_a_wins', 'model_b_wins', 'ties', and 'avg_gap' (mean absolute score difference), and `dominates()` that returns True only if one model wins ALL benchmarks.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
class ModelComparator:
    def __init__(self, model_a: str, model_b: str):
        self.model_a = model_a
        self.model_b = model_b
        self.benchmarks = {}

    def add_benchmark(self, name: str, model_a_score: float,
                      model_b_score: float):
        # Store the benchmark name and both scores
        pass

    def get_winner(self, benchmark_name: str) -> str:
        # Return model name with higher score, or 'tie'
        pass

    def summary(self) -> dict:
        # Return dict with model_a_wins, model_b_wins, ties, avg_gap
        # avg_gap = mean of absolute differences across benchmarks
        pass

    def dominates(self) -> bool:
        # True if one model wins every benchmark (no ties, no losses)
        pass

# Test it:
comp = ModelComparator("bert-base", "roberta-base")
comp.add_benchmark("SST-2", 92.7, 94.8)
comp.add_benchmark("MNLI", 84.6, 87.6)
comp.add_benchmark("QNLI", 90.5, 92.8)
comp.add_benchmark("QQP", 89.2, 89.2)

print(comp.get_winner("SST-2"))
print(comp.get_winner("QQP"))

s = comp.summary()
print(s["model_a_wins"])
print(s["model_b_wins"])
print(s["ties"])
print(f"{s['avg_gap']:.2f}")
print(comp.dominates())

en: Store benchmarks as a dict mapping name to (model_a_score, model_b_score) tuples
In get_winner(), compare the two scores — return self.model_a, self.model_b, or 'tie' based on which is greater or if they're equal
For avg_gap, compute sum(abs(a - b) for a, b in benchmarks.values()) / len(benchmarks)

practice_challenges.test_code:
c = ModelComparator("A", "B")
c.add_benchmark("t1", 90.0, 95.0)
c.add_benchmark("t2", 88.0, 92.0)
assert c.get_winner("t1") == "B"
assert c.get_winner("t2") == "B"
s = c.summary()
assert s["model_a_wins"] == 0
assert s["model_b_wins"] == 2
assert s["ties"] == 0
assert abs(s["avg_gap"] - 4.5) < 0.01
assert c.dominates() == True
c.add_benchmark("t3", 95.0, 95.0)
assert c.dominates() == False
assert c.get_winner("t3") == "tie"
s2 = c.summary()
assert s2["ties"] == 1

instruction.en: Build a `TokenizerAnalyzer` class that compares how two different tokenizers handle text. Initialize with two tokenizer configs (each a dict with 'name', 'vocab_size', and 'cased' boolean). Implement `tokenize(text, tokenizer_name)` that simulates tokenization by splitting on whitespace, lowercasing if uncased, and splitting tokens longer than 6 characters into subwords (first 4 chars + '##' + rest). Implement `compare(text)` that returns a dict with each tokenizer's token count, whether either produced an unknown token, and which has fewer tokens. Implement `batch_compare(texts)` that returns average token counts for each tokenizer.

en: Look up the tokenizer config by matching tokenizer_name against self.tokenizer_a['name'] or self.tokenizer_b['name']
For subword splitting: if len(token) > 6, yield token[:4] and '##' + token[4:] as two separate tokens in the result list
For batch_compare, call compare() on each text and average the a_tokens and b_tokens counts

practice_challenges.test_code:
bc = {'name': 'bert', 'vocab_size': 30000, 'cased': False}
rc = {'name': 'roberta', 'vocab_size': 50000, 'cased': True}
a = TokenizerAnalyzer(bc, rc)
t1 = a.tokenize("Transformer", "bert")
assert t1 == ['tran', '##sformer']
t2 = a.tokenize("Transformer", "roberta")
assert t2 == ['Tran', '##sformer']
t3 = a.tokenize("Hi there", "bert")
assert t3 == ['hi', 'there']
t4 = a.tokenize("Hi there", "roberta")
assert t4 == ['Hi', 'there']
r = a.compare("understanding language")
assert r['a_tokens'] == 4
assert r['b_tokens'] == 4
assert r['fewer_tokens'] == 'tie'
b = a.batch_compare(['Hi', 'understanding'])
assert abs(b['a_avg'] - 1.5) < 0.01
assert abs(b['b_avg'] - 1.5) < 0.01
